perturbation.original_text: Seller, Key Personnel and Shareholder covenant and agree that each shall not for a period of one (1) year, communicate or divulge to, or use for the benefit of itself or any other person, firm, association or corporation, any information in any way relating to the Proprietary Property, in competition with the business of the Company.
perturbation.changed_text: Seller, Key Personnel and Shareholder covenant and agree that each shall not for a period of one (1) year, communicate or divulge to, or use for the benefit of itself or any other person, firm, association or corporation, any information in any way relating to the Proprietary Property, regardless of whether such use is in competition with the business of the Company.
perturbation.explanation: The original text restricts the use of Proprietary Property 'in competition with the business of the Company.' The modified text broadens the restriction to prohibit any use of Proprietary Property, even if it does not compete with the Company's business. This creates an **in-text contradiction** if other sections assume competition is a necessary condition for breach.
perturbation.contradicted_text: Seller, Key Personnel and Shareholder understand that the provisions of this Agreement contain restrictive covenants and prohibit the disclosure of the Proprietary Property of the Company, agree to the reasonability of said provisions, and do herewith expressly agree and acknowledge that their breach of this Agreement will not be adequately compensated by money damages. Seller, Key Personnel and Shareholder acknowledge that the restrictions contained in this Agreement are a reasonable and necessary protection of the legitimate interests of the Company and that any violation of these restrictions would cause substantial irreparable injury to the Company. Seller, Key Personnel and Shareholder acknowledge that Purchaser would not have entered into this Agreement without receiving the consideration offered by Seller and Shareholder in binding itself to these restrictions.
perturbation.justification: The original text prohibits the use of Proprietary Property 'in competition with the business of the Company.' The modified text prohibits any use of Proprietary Property, even if it does not compete with the Company's business. The contradicted text states that 'the restrictions contained in this Agreement are a reasonable and necessary protection of the legitimate interests of the Company and that any violation of these restrictions would cause substantial irreparable injury to the Company.' By removing the 'competition' element, the modified text now covers situations where the use of Proprietary Property might not harm the company, contradicting the statement that any violation would cause substantial irreparable injury. This aligns with the definition of 'Ambiguities - In Text Contradiction' as it introduces conflicting obligations.

perturbation.original_text: Seller, Key Personnel and Shareholder, jointly and severally, expressly covenant and agree that for a period of one (1) year, Seller will not engage in any business or perform any service, directly or indirectly, in competition with the business of the Company, or have any interest, whether as proprietor, partner, employee, stockholder, principal, agent, consultant, director, officer, or in any other capacity or manner whatsoever, in any enterprise that shall engage in the business of the Company, except through publicly-traded shares of a corporation or mutual fund listed on a major stock exchange.
perturbation.changed_text: Seller, Key Personnel and Shareholder, jointly and severally, expressly covenant and agree that for a period of one (1) year, Seller will not engage in any business that directly and explicitly undermines the Company's core operational functions, or have any controlling interest (greater than 50% ownership) in any enterprise that directly competes with the business of the Company, except through publicly-traded shares of a corporation or mutual fund listed on a major stock exchange.
perturbation.explanation: The original text prohibits any competition, direct or indirect. The modified text narrows the scope, only prohibiting business that 'directly and explicitly undermines' the company or having 'controlling interest'. This introduces an **in-text contradiction** because other sections rely on the broad definition of 'competition' from the original.
perturbation.contradicted_text: Seller, Key Personnel and Shareholder covenant and agree that each shall not for a period of one (1) year, communicate or divulge to, or use for the benefit of itself or any other person, firm, association or corporation, any information in any way relating to the Proprietary Property, in competition with the business of the Company.
perturbation.justification: The original text broadly prohibits any competition with the Company. The changed text limits the restriction to business that 'directly and explicitly undermines' the company's operational functions or having 'controlling interest.' This creates an in-text contradiction with the 'Contradicted Text,' which prohibits using Proprietary Property 'in competition with the business of the Company.' The original version of the first clause would have prevented someone from using Proprietary Property in *any* competition, but the changed version allows for use of Proprietary Property in business that competes so long as it does not 'directly and explicitly undermine' core operational functions and does not involve 'controlling interest'. The modified text therefore creates a direct contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: In furtherance of the foregoing and not in limitation thereof, Key Personnel and Shareholder agree that for a period of one (1) year, Key Personnel and Shareholder shall not (aa) directly or indirectly, solicit or service in any way, on behalf of itself or on behalf of or in conjunction with others, any customers, or prospective customers who have been solicited or serviced by the Company; (bb) directly or indirectly take any action which may induce any customer or divert any business from the Company; or (cc) directly or indirectly, for himself or any enterprise engaged in competition with the Company, solicit for employment or employ any employee who is then employed by the Company or who has been employed by the Company within one (1) year prior to the termination of his employment.
perturbation.changed_text: In furtherance of the foregoing and not in limitation thereof, Key Personnel and Shareholder agree that for a period of one (1) year, Key Personnel and Shareholder shall not (aa) directly solicit or service key clients of the Company, defined as those generating at least 20% of annual revenue; (bb) directly take actions proven to intentionally induce key customers to leave the Company; or (cc) directly solicit employees in leadership positions with specialized technical knowledge, who are then employed by the Company or who have been employed by the Company within one (1) year prior to the termination of his employment.
perturbation.explanation: The original text has a broad restriction on soliciting any customer, prospective customer, or employee. The modified text narrows these restrictions to only key clients, intentionally induced customers and leadership position employees with specialized knowledge. This is an **in-text contradiction** with the remedies outlined in section 3.d (ii) which does not qualify customer type.
perturbation.contradicted_text: Seller, Key Personnel and Shareholder expressly agree that in the event of any suit which may be brought by the Company for any violation of the provisions of this Agreement, any such breach or threatened breach of this Agreement shall entitle the Company to any and/or of the following remedies: (aa) an order in any such suit enjoining Seller, Key Personnel and Shareholder from violating said provisions. An order to that effect may be entered at any stage of such litigation, without the requirement to post bond, and any application for such injunction shall be without prejudice to any other right of action which may accrue to the parties by reason of the breach or threatened breach of this Agreement; and (bb) an order in any such suit providing for monetary damages.
perturbation.justification: The changed text narrows the scope of restrictions on solicitation compared to the original text. The original text broadly prohibits soliciting any customer, prospective customer, or employee, while the changed text limits these restrictions to key clients, customers intentionally induced to leave, and employees in leadership positions with specialized knowledge. This creates a contradiction because the contradicted text refers to 'any violation of the provisions of this Agreement' and allows for injunctive relief and monetary damages without specifying or limiting the type of customer or employee. Therefore, if the company sues for soliciting a non-key customer, the changed text would contradict the remedies section, creating an in-text contradiction as the remedies provided in the contradicted text are not compatible with the limitations introduced in the changed text, which specifies customer type.

perturbation.original_text: Seller, Key Personnel and Shareholder acknowledge that the restrictions contained in this Agreement are a reasonable and necessary protection of the legitimate interests of the Company and that any violation of these restrictions would cause substantial irreparable injury to the Company. Seller, Key Personnel and Shareholder acknowledge that Purchaser would not have entered into this Agreement without receiving the consideration offered by Seller and Shareholder in binding itself to these restrictions.
perturbation.changed_text: Seller, Key Personnel and Shareholder believe that the restrictions contained in this Agreement are generally aligned with the legitimate interests of the Company, and that any significant violation of these restrictions could potentially cause injury to the Company, but monetary compensation should be sufficient. Seller, Key Personnel and Shareholder acknowledge that Purchaser would still have likely entered into this Agreement, even without these restrictions, but would have adjusted the purchase price.
perturbation.explanation: The original text affirms that any violation would cause 'substantial irreparable injury' and that the purchaser relied on the restrictions. The changed text states that only significant violations *could potentially* cause injury, monetary compensation would be sufficient, and the Purchaser likely would have entered the agreement anyway. This is an **in-text contradiction** as section 3.d. (ii) references monetary damages will be possible, contradicting the phrase of 'sufficient'.
perturbation.contradicted_text: Seller, Key Personnel and Shareholder expressly agree that in the event of any suit which may be brought by the Company for any violation of the provisions of this Agreement, any such breach or threatened breach of this Agreement shall entitle the Company to any and/or of the following remedies: (aa) an order in any such suit enjoining Seller, Key Personnel and Shareholder from violating said provisions. An order to that effect may be entered at any stage of such litigation, without the requirement to post bond, and any application for such injunction shall be without prejudice to any other right of action which may accrue to the parties by reason of the breach or threatened breach of this Agreement; and (bb) an order in any such suit providing for monetary damages.
perturbation.justification: The changed text modifies the original agreement by stating that monetary compensation should be sufficient for violations, contradicting the contradicted text which allows for injunctive relief (i.e., an order to stop the violating behavior). The original text's assertion of 'substantial irreparable injury' necessitating injunctive relief is directly undermined by the changed text suggesting monetary compensation suffices, creating a clear conflict regarding available remedies. The Perturbation explanation states that section 3.d.(ii) references monetary damages will be possible, contradicting the phrase of 'sufficient'.

perturbation.original_text: The covenants on the part of Key Personnel and Shareholder contained in this Agreement shall be construed as an agreement independent of any other provision in this Agreement or the SPA. The existence of any claim or cause of action of Seller and/or Shareholder against the Company or Purchaser, whether predicated on this Agreement, the SPA or otherwise shall not constitute a defense to the enforcement of this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: The covenants on the part of Key Personnel and Shareholder contained in this Agreement shall be interpreted in conjunction with all other relevant provisions in this Agreement and the SPA, considering the overall context of the transaction. The existence of any material claim or cause of action of Seller and/or Shareholder against the Company or Purchaser, directly arising from a breach of the SPA by the Company, may be considered by a court as a mitigating factor when determining the appropriate remedy for any alleged breach of this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: The original text states the covenants are independent, and no claim can be a defense. The modified text says the covenants should be interpreted in conjunction and a claim arising from a breach *may* be considered. This is an **in-text contradiction** related to defense on claims.
perturbation.contradicted_text: If any party commences an action against another party to enforce any of the terms, covenants, conditions or provisions of this Agreement, or because of a breach by a party of its obligations under this Agreement, the prevailing party in any such action shall be entitled to recover its losses, including reasonable attorneys’ fees, costs and interest incurred in connection with the enforcement of this agreement.
perturbation.justification: The original text created an independent agreement, where no claim could be a defense. The changed text now says the covenants should be interpreted in conjunction and a claim arising from a breach may be considered as a mitigating factor. This contradicts the contradicted text, which states that the prevailing party enforcing the agreement shall be entitled to recover its losses. The changed text introduces the possibility of a mitigating factor (breach of SPA), potentially reducing the losses recoverable by the prevailing party. This creates a clear and direct conflict, fulfilling the definition of 'Ambiguities – In Text Contradiction'.

perturbation.original_text: All notices, consents, waivers, requests and other communications under this Agreement must be in writing to: (i) SELLER/ Troy Holdings International, Inc. SHAREHOLDER/ Attn: Scott MacCannell, President KEY PERSONNEL: Ana Misra, Vice President 204-11 Cidermill Avenue Vaughan, Ontario, L4K 4B6, Canada E-mail: smaccannell@troyinc.ca amisra@troyinc.ca
perturbation.changed_text: All notices, consents, waivers, requests and other communications under this Agreement may be delivered electronically via email to: (i) SELLER/ Troy Holdings International, Inc. SHAREHOLDER/ Attn: Scott MacCannell, President KEY PERSONNEL: Ana Misra, Vice President E-mail: smaccannell@troyinc.ca amisra@troyinc.ca. For formal legal notices requiring acknowledgment, physical delivery to 204-11 Cidermill Avenue Vaughan, Ontario, L4K 4B6, Canada is required.
perturbation.explanation: The original text mandates that 'all' notices must be 'in writing'. The modified text allows email for general communication but requires physical delivery for formal notices. This is an **in-text contradiction** as section 4 states that everything must be in writting, so can be seen as not valid under the agreement if it is sent by email.
perturbation.contradicted_text: Except as otherwise provided in this Agreement, all such communications shall be deemed to have been duly given: a. in the case of a notice sent by regular or registered or certified mail, three business days after it is duly deposited in the mails; b. in the case of a notice delivered by hand, when personally delivered; c. in the case of a notice sent by facsimile, upon transmission subject to telephone confirmation of receipt; and d. in the case of a notice sent by overnight mail or overnight courier service, the next business day after such notice is mailed or delivered to such courier, in each case given or addressed as aforesaid.
perturbation.justification: The original text mandates all notices be in writing. The changed text allows email for general notices, but requires physical delivery for formal legal notices, contradicting the original requirement for all notices to be in writing, as highlighted in the Perturbation Explanation. This directly conflicts with the contradicted text, which outlines how communications are deemed given based on delivery method (mail, hand, fax, courier) under the assumption they are all in writing, creating a strong contradiction as to what is an appropriate form of communication under the agreement.
